system_prompt: You are an OpenFOAM debugging planner. Given current foam files, error logs and reviewer analysis, create a minimal rewrite plan. Output MUST be strict JSON only, with this exact schema: {"target_files": [{"file": "relative/path", "changes": "change1; change2"}]}. Rules: 1) Do not use markdown, backticks, or comments. 2) Use double quotes for all strings. 3) In changes, use short plain text actions separated by semicolons. 4) Do not include parentheses, backticks, or quote characters inside changes text. 5) Do not include run steps; only file edits.

startFrom       startTime;

startTime       0;

stopAt          endTime;

endTime         500;

deltaT          1;

writeControl    timeStep;

writeInterval   100;

purgeWrite      0;

writeFormat     ascii;

writePrecision  6;

writeCompression off;

timeFormat      general;

timePrecision   6;

runTimeModifiable true;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='decomposeParDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvConstraints', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile

    format      ascii;

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //


// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSchemes', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSchemes;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

ddtSchemes
{
    default         steadyState;
}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
    grad(U)         cellLimited Gauss linear 1;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;
    div(phi,U)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind grad(U);
    div(phi,e)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,h)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,K)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,k)      bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,epsilon) bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(phi,Ekp)    bounded Gauss linearUpwind default;
    div(((rho*nuEff)*dev2(T(grad(U))))) Gauss linear;
}

laplacianSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

interpolationSchemes
{
    default         linear;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

wallDist
{
    method          meshWave;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    p
    {
        solver          GAMG;
        smoother        GaussSeidel;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(U|h|e|k|epsilon|omega)"
    {
        solver          smoothSolver;
        smoother        symGaussSeidel;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }
}

SIMPLE
{
    nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 0;
    pMinFactor      0.1;
    pMaxFactor      1.5;

    residualControl
    {
        p               1e-4;
        U               1e-4;
        "(h|e)"         1e-4;
        k               1e-4;
        epsilon         1e-4;
    }
}

relaxationFactors
{
    fields
    {
        p               0.3;
        rho             0.01;
    }
    equations
    {
        U               0.7;
        "(h|e)"         0.7;
        k               0.7;
        epsilon         0.7;
    }
}

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

scale   1;

vertices
(
    // Block 0 - inlet section (straight duct along x-axis)

    (-50  25  25)   // 0
    (-50  25 -25)   // 1
    (-50 -25 -25)   // 2
    (-50 -25  25)   // 3

    (  0  25  25)   // 4
    (  0  25 -25)   // 5
    (  0 -25 -25)   // 6
    (  0 -25  25)   // 7

    // Block 1 - bend section

    ( 25   0 -25)   // 9
    ( 25 -50 -25)   // 10
    ( 25 -50  25)   // 11

    // Block 2 - outlet straight section

    ( 25  -75 -25)   // 13
    ( 25 -125 -25)   // 14
    ( 25 -125  25)   // 15

    (-500  -75  25)   // 16
    (-500  -75 -25)   // 17
    (-500 -125 -25)   // 18
    (-500 -125  25)   // 19
);

blocks
(
    // Block 0: Inlet straight section
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7) (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 1: Bend section
    hex (8 9 10 11 12 13 14 15) (10 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)

    // Block 2: Outlet straight section
    hex (12 13 14 15 16 17 18 19) (100 10 10) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);

edges
(
);

boundary
(
    inlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 4 5 1)
        );
    }

    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (16 19 18 17)
        );
    }

    upperWall
    {

        faces
        (
            (0 4 5 1)
            (4 8 9 5)
            (8 12 13 9)
            (12 16 17 13)
        );
    }

    lowerWall
    {

        faces
        (
            (3 7 6 2)
            (3 2 10 11)
            (15 19 18 14)
        );
    }

    leftWall

        faces

            (7 3 11 8)
            (11 8 12 15)
            (15 12 16 19)
        );
    }

    rightWall

        faces
        (
            (1 5 6 2)
            (5 9 10 6)
            (9 13 14 10)
            (13 17 18 14)
        );
    }
);

mergePatchPairs
(
);

<review_analysis># Error Analysis and Resolution

## Error 1: `log.blockMesh` — Block 0 is still "inside-out"

The previous attempt tried reversing the vertex order, but the fundamental issue is that the **vertex coordinates themselves** define a non-convex/curved geometry where the standard hex block vertex ordering doesn't produce valid convex blocks. Let me examine the geometry more carefully.

Looking at the current `blockMeshDict`:

Vertices 0-3 are at x=-50 (a rectangle from y=-25..25, z=-25..25).
Vertices 4-7 are at x=0 (same y,z range).

So Block 0 is a proper rectangular prism — it should work with `hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)`. The problem is that the **face definitions on the boundary** don't match the internal block connectivity.

Actually, let me trace through Block 0: `hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)`
- Bottom face (0,1,2,3) at x=-50
- Top face (4,5,6,7) at x=0

Checking the boundary faces:
- `inlet`: `(0 4 5 1)` — this is the face at x=-50, but the vertices are ordered as (0,4,5,1) which goes from bottom-left to top-right on the bottom face. For a face on the x=-50 plane, looking from outside inward, the normal should point in the **-x direction**. The ordering (0→1→2→3) on the bottom face has a normal pointing **+x** (right-hand rule). So to get a **-x** normal for the inlet face, we need the **opposite** winding: **(1 5 4 0)** or equivalently **(0 4 5 1)** depending on perspective.

Wait — actually checking the current dict: the inlet face is `(0 4 5 1)`. On the bottom face (0,1,2,3), the edge from 0→1 runs in +y. Using right-hand rule with thumb in +y, fingers curl from 0→4 (which is +z? No, 0=(−50,25,25), 4=(0,25,25) — that's +x). Actually I need to be more systematic.

Let me recompute properly. For `hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)`:
- Face 0-1-2-3 (bottom) normal = (1-0)×(2-0) = (0,0,-50) × (−50,0,-50) = ... actually the standard convention is that face 0-1-2-3 has normal pointing from face 0-1-2-3 toward face 4-5-6-7.

The real issue is likely that the **boundary face definitions don't match the block's internal face orientations**. Each hex block has 6 faces, and the boundary faces must use the exact vertex ordering that matches how that face appears in the block.

## Recommended Fix

Redefine the blocks with explicit face definitions that clearly show the correspondence. The simplest approach is to **reorder the vertices in each hex block** so that the face definitions align correctly.

Here’s a proven working approach for this kind of L-shaped duct:

- **Block 0** (inlet section): `hex (0 3 2 1 4 7 6 5)` — this reverses the winding so that the face at x=-50 (vertices 0,1,2,3) has the correct outward normal direction matching the boundary face `(0 4 5 1)`

Actually, let me look at what the boundary face `(0 4 5 1)` represents. These are the faces connecting vertices at x=-50 to x=0 at y=+25. Looking at the current block definition `hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)`, the face between vertices 0,1,4,5 is indeed the top face of the bottom half — but the ordering matters.

The cleanest fix: **change Block 0 to `hex (0 4 5 1 3 7 6 2)`**. This swaps the two pairs so that the face at x=-50 becomes `(0 3 2 1)` which winds correctly for the inlet boundary face `(0 4 5 1)` when viewed from outside.

No — let me just systematically fix this. The core problem is that the boundary face `(0 4 5 1)` connects the face at x=-50 (vertices 0,1,2,3) to the face at x=0 (vertices 4,5,6,7) along their shared edge. For `hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7)`, the face at x=-50 is 0-1-2-3. To expose only the face 0-1 on that boundary, the block definition needs to make that face correspond to the external face.

The proven solution: **Change Block 0 to `hex (0 3 2 1 4 7 6 5)`**. This reverses the winding of the bottom face so that the face visible at x=-50 is 0-1-2-3 with the correct orientation for the boundary face `(0 4 5 1)`.

Do the same for the other blocks:

- **Block 1**: Change to `hex (7 11 10 6 3 15 14 2)`
- **Block 2**: Change to `hex (12 19 18 13 15 16 17 14)`

Also update the boundary face definitions to match the new block topology. For example, the `inlet` face should be updated to match whatever face is now exposed at x=-50.

## Error 2: `log.rhoSimpleFoam` — Cascading error (will resolve once mesh is built)

## Additional Note

After fixing `blockMeshDict`, verify that the patch names in all `0/` field files exactly match those in `blockMeshDict`: `inlet`, `outlet`, `upperWall`, `lowerWall`, `leftWall`, `rightWall`. Ensure no leftover references to `frontAndBack`, `innerWall`, or `outerWall`.</review_analysis>

Follow the same geomtery as the tutorial. Use a start time of 0 seconds, an end time of 500 seconds, a time step of 1 second, and write results every 100 seconds. Use a massflow rate inlet of 0.5 with turbulentBL profile. Outlet is pressureInletOutletVelocity. Temperature has an inlet value of 1000. Pressure inlet has a value of 110000. use kepsilon turbulence model. Use rhoSimpleFoam solver. Use Pr value of 1.0.</user_requirement>
